literal: everything exists; the foundational thesis of the Sarvāstivāda — past, present, and future dharmas all exist as such, in their respective temporal modes
invariant_definition: The foundational thesis of the Sarvāstivāda-Vaibhāṣika school of Buddhist Abhidharma, articulated technically in the Mahā-Vibhāṣā and systematised in Vasubandhu's Abhidharma-Kośa-Bhāṣya (orthodox version) and defended in Saṅghabhadra's Nyāyānusāra. The thesis is *not* the trivial claim that everything exists; it is the technical claim that *dharmas* (the irreducible constituents of experience) exist as real (*dravyasat*), momentary (*kṣaṇika*) constituents in three temporal modes (*adhva*) — past, present, and future — with their *svabhāva* (own-being) invariant across the modes. The decisive clarification, due to Vasumitra and accepted by Saṅghabhadra as the school's settled position, is *avasthā-anyathātva*: only the temporal mode (*avasthā*) varies across past / present / future; the *svabhāva* of the *dharma* is the same. Causal efficacy (*kāritra*) is restricted to the present-mode *dharma*; past and future *dharma*s are real but causally inactive. The position is constructed to satisfy two desiderata: it explains why memory and prediction have determinate intentional objects (the past and future *dharma*s they refer to are real), and it explains why only present causes have effects (only the present mode bears *kāritra*). The doctrine is the principal target of the Vedāntic refutation at Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.18–20 and of the Sautrāntika critique within Vasubandhu's own Kośa-Bhāṣya.

per_school.definition: *Sarvam asti* is the technical claim that *dharma*s exist as real (*dravyasat*), momentary (*kṣaṇika*) constituents in three temporal modes, with the *svabhāva* invariant across modes (Vasumitra's *avasthā-anyathātva*). Causal efficacy (*kāritra*) is restricted to the present-mode *dharma*. The doctrine is articulated in the Mahā-Vibhāṣā, summarised in Vasubandhu's Kośa-Kārikā 5.25–27, and defended in Saṅghabhadra's Nyāyānusāra against the Sautrāntika critique that real-but-inefficacious is incoherent.
primary_loci: Mahā-Vibhāṣā (selected passages on the four ācāryas' accounts of how dharmas exist in the three modes)

per_school.definition: The Sautrāntika critique, advanced in Vasubandhu's auto-commentary on the Kośa-Kārikās, denies *sarvam asti* as a coherent metaphysical position. Only the present moment exists as *dravyasat*; past and future are conceptual constructions on the seeds (*bīja*) deposited in the cognitive series. The critique anticipates the Yogācāra *ālaya-vijñāna* doctrine and supplies the philosophical motive for Vasubandhu's later move to Yogācāra.

The Vedāntic critique at Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.18–20 (Śaṅkara's Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya thereon and Madhva's Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya and Anu-Vyākhyāna thereon) targets *sarvam asti* together with *kṣaṇika-vāda* (momentariness) on which it rests. The principal Vedāntic objection is that the momentary-dharma ontology cannot account for recognition (*pratyabhijñā*) across moments without smuggling in the very kind of persisting substrate it denies; the doctrine of momentary *dharma*s with *svabhāva* therefore collapses under the same considerations Vasubandhu's own Sautrāntika gloss had already begun to press.

translator_note: Translating *sarvam asti* as 'everything exists' is technically correct but obscures the precise commitment. The doctrine is *not* the trivial claim that everything is real; it is the technical claim that *dharma*s exist in three temporal modes with their *svabhāva* invariant across modes, with causal efficacy restricted to the present mode. Western readers should not collapse the position into either pan-temporal eternalism or four-dimensionalist eternalism: the Sarvāstivāda *dharma*s are momentary in the sense that their causal efficacy is restricted to a single present moment, but they are temporally extended in the sense that they exist in past and future modes as well as in the present. Cox 1995 (Disputed Dharmas) is the principal modern study; do not flatten the doctrine to 'everything exists'.
